pi_items.source: https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/unna/1402 — UN 1402 = Calcium carbide, Hazard Class 4.3 (Dangerous When Wet)
https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/unna/1402 — confirmed Calcium carbide

pi_items.verified_value: Acetylene gas (C2H2) and Calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)2). Reaction is exothermic — enough heat may ignite the acetylene.
pi_items.source: CAMEO Chemicals datasheet https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/chemical/2769 Air & Water Reactions section: 'Reacts rapidly with water to generate the flammable gas acetylene and the base calcium hydroxide. Enough heat may be generated to ignite the gas.' Balanced equation: CaC2 + 2H2O -> Ca(OH)2 + C2H2
https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/unna/1428 — UN 1428 = Sodium, Hazard Class 4.3 (Dangerous When Wet). DOT proper shipping name is simply 'Sodium'.
https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/unna/1428 — confirmed Sodium

pi_items.verified_value: Hydrogen gas (H2) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH). Reaction is violent — hydrogen ignites spontaneously.
pi_items.source: CAMEO Chemicals datasheet https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/chemical/7794 Air & Water Reactions section: 'Reacts violently with water to give sodium hydroxide and hydrogen, which ignites spontaneously.' Balanced equation: 2Na + 2H2O -> 2NaOH + H2
https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/unna/1809 — UN 1809 = Phosphorus trichloride, Hazard Class 6.1 with subsidiary 8, Inhalation Hazard Zone B
https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/unna/1809 — confirmed Phosphorus trichloride

pi_items.metadata_value: Hydrogen Chloride gas (HCl) and phosphorous acid
pi_items.verified_value: Hydrogen Chloride gas (HCl) and phosphorous acid (H3PO3). Reaction is violent — produces dense HCl vapors.
pi_items.source: CAMEO Chemicals datasheet https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/chemical/4249 Air & Water Reactions section: 'Fumes in air. Reacts violently with water. Hydrolysis produces dense hydrogen chloride vapors.' Balanced equation: PCl3 + 3H2O -> H3PO3 + 3HCl. Phosphorous acid (H3PO3) confirmed via Wikipedia and Princeton EHS.
https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/unna/1005 — UN 1005 = Ammonia, anhydrous, Hazard Class 2.3 with subsidiary 8
https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/unna/1005 — confirmed Ammonia, anhydrous

pi_items.verified_value: Sodium Hypochlorite (DOT proper shipping name: 'Hypochlorite solutions')
pi_items.source: https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/unna/1791 — UN 1791 = Hypochlorite solutions, Hazard Class 8. Sodium Hypochlorite solution falls under this broader category.
https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/unna/1791 — confirmed Hypochlorite solutions

pi_items.metadata_value: HCl gas from PCl3 hydrolysis reacts with Sodium Hypochlorite to produce Chlorine gas (Cl2)
pi_items.verified_value: HCl + NaOCl -> NaCl + Cl2 + H2O (with 2:1 stoichiometry: NaOCl + 2HCl -> NaCl + Cl2 + H2O). This is the well-known acid-bleach reaction producing toxic chlorine gas.
pi_items.source: ChemicalAid equation balancer; ACSH article documenting fatal Cl2 release from acid + bleach mixing (2019 restaurant incident); standard inorganic chemistry. CAMEO Reactivity Prediction tool accessible at https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/reactivity would flag this as incompatible.

pi_items.metadata_value: HCl gas reacts with Anhydrous Ammonia to produce Ammonium Chloride aerosol (NH4Cl, dense white cloud)
pi_items.verified_value: HCl(g) + NH3(g) -> NH4Cl(s). Forms a dense white aerosol of fine solid ammonium chloride particles suspended in air. Classic demonstration reaction.
pi_items.source: RSC Education diffusion experiment; ScienceNotes white smoke demonstration; standard chemistry. The 'dense white cloud' description is accurate — NH4Cl forms as fine solid particles in air.
https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/unna/1689 — UN 1689 = Sodium cyanide, solid, Hazard Class 6.1, ERG Guide 157
https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/unna/1689 — confirmed Sodium cyanide, solid

pi_items.verified_value: Sulfuric Acid (with more than 51 percent acid)
pi_items.source: https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/unna/1830 — UN 1830 = Sulfuric acid with more than 51% acid, Hazard Class 8, PG II. The scenario specifies 98% concentration, which falls under UN 1830.
https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/unna/1830 — confirmed Sulfuric acid

pi_items.metadata_value: Sulfuric Acid + Sodium Cyanide produces Hydrogen Cyanide gas (HCN) if containers breach during flooding
pi_items.verified_value: H2SO4 + 2NaCN -> 2HCN(g) + Na2SO4. HCN is acutely toxic (boiling point 25.6C, readily volatilizes). This is a strong-acid-displaces-weak-acid reaction. Lethal at low ppm concentrations.
pi_items.source: Standard acid-base displacement chemistry. ChemicalAid balanced equation. HCN boiling point from PubChem/Wikipedia. This reaction is the basis of historical cyanide poisoning incidents.

pi_items.verified_value: Chlorine (Cl2) is the worst-case chemical for the PRIMARY cascade chain (Alpha water reactions -> HCl -> Beta NaOCl -> Cl2). Note: HCN at Gamma is also extremely dangerous but requires a separate flooding event at Gamma.
pi_items.source: Assessment based on ERG protective action distances: Chlorine (ERG 124) has PAD of 7.0+ miles for large spill night conditions. Chlorine is a TIH (Toxic Inhalation Hazard) material. While HCN (ERG 117) is also extremely toxic, Cl2 production is the direct cascade consequence of Alpha flooding, making it the primary worst case.
https://cameochemicals.noaa.gov/unna/1017 — Chlorine UN 1017 = ERG Guide 124 'Gases - Toxic and/or Corrosive - Oxidizing'. Confirmed via NOAA CAMEO Chemicals and PHMSA ERG 2020.

Verified during live environment testing: directory /home/ga/Documents/ exists and is writable by user ga. setup_task.sh creates the directory and deletes any pre-existing file. export_result.sh checks this exact path.

Verified during live environment testing: file is copied from /workspace/data/flood_hazard_scenario.txt to /home/ga/Desktop/ by setup_task.sh. File size 6889 bytes, contains all 3 facility descriptions, 7 chemicals with UN numbers, weather data, population data, and assignment instructions.

privileged_info_summary: This task requires the agent to assess cascading chemical hazards across three facilities during a flood event using CAMEO Chemicals. All 7 chemicals and UN numbers are verified against CAMEO: Calcium Carbide (UN 1402), Sodium (UN 1428), Phosphorus Trichloride (UN 1809), Anhydrous Ammonia (UN 1005), Sodium Hypochlorite/Hypochlorite solutions (UN 1791), Sodium Cyanide (UN 1689), Sulfuric Acid (UN 1830). WATER REACTION PRODUCTS at Facility Alpha: (1) CaC2 + 2H2O -> Ca(OH)2 + C2H2 (acetylene gas, flammable, exothermic — CAMEO says 'enough heat may be generated to ignite the gas'); (2) 2Na + 2H2O -> 2NaOH + H2 (hydrogen gas, violent reaction — CAMEO says 'reacts violently with water, hydrogen ignites spontaneously'); (3) PCl3 + 3H2O -> H3PO3 + 3HCl (hydrogen chloride gas/dense vapors — CAMEO says 'reacts violently with water, hydrolysis produces dense hydrogen chloride vapors'). KEY CASCADE at Facility Beta: HCl gas drifting from Alpha reacts with Sodium Hypochlorite at Beta via NaOCl + 2HCl -> NaCl + Cl2 + H2O, producing toxic CHLORINE GAS. Additionally, HCl + NH3 -> NH4Cl (ammonium chloride), forming a dense white aerosol cloud. FACILITY GAMMA RISK: H2SO4 + 2NaCN -> 2HCN + Na2SO4 produces lethal Hydrogen Cyanide gas if containers breach. ERG Guide for Chlorine is 124 ('Gases - Toxic and/or Corrosive - Oxidizing'). Chlorine large-spill protective action distances from CAMEO ERG data: 7.0+ miles for night conditions (Table 3/rail scenario) or approximately 5.0 miles (Table 1 general). The overall recommendation should be EVACUATION of the corridor given the scale of potential Cl2 release and HCN risk at Gamma. The scenario file at ~/Desktop/flood_hazard_scenario.txt is 6889 bytes, contains all facility data with UN numbers and weather conditions (NW wind at 8 mph pushing releases toward downstream facilities SE). The output must be saved to ~/Documents/flood_cascade_assessment.txt.
